mcqs.question: Two states p and q in a DFA are said to be equivalent if:

options.text: For some input string w, both p and q accept w
For all input strings w, both p and q either accept or reject w
For no input string w, both p and q accept w
For exactly one input string w, both p and q accept w

mcqs.correctAnswer: For all input strings w, both p and q either accept or reject w
mcqs.explanation: Equivalent states (indistinguishable) produce the same acceptance/rejection behavior for every possible input string. If any string distinguishes them, they are distinguishable.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is the first step in minimizing a DFA?

options.text: Partition states into accepting and non-accepting groups

mcqs.correctAnswer: Partition states into accepting and non-accepting groups
mcqs.explanation: The initial partition puts all accepting states in one set and all non-accepting states in another set. This is because an accepting state can never be equivalent to a non-accepting state.

mcqs.question: A dead state in a DFA is:

options.text: A non-accepting state that loops to itself on all inputs
A state with no outgoing transitions

mcqs.correctAnswer: A non-accepting state that loops to itself on all inputs
mcqs.explanation: A dead or trap state is a non-final state that, for every input symbol, transitions to itself. It represents automatic rejection of any string that leads to it.

mcqs.question: The minimization algorithm terminates when:

options.text: All states are in one group

mcqs.correctAnswer: The partition stops changing
mcqs.explanation: The algorithm iteratively refines the partition. Termination occurs when two consecutive partitions are identical, meaning no more states can be distinguished.

mcqs.question: In the context of Myhill-Nerode theorem, the number of states in the minimal DFA equals:

options.text: The number of strings in the language
The number of equivalence classes of the language
The length of the longest string

mcqs.correctAnswer: The number of equivalence classes of the language
mcqs.explanation: Myhill-Nerode theorem states that the minimum number of states in any DFA recognizing a regular language equals the number of distinct equivalence classes of that language.

mcqs.question: Which method is used for DFA minimization?
mcqs.correctAnswer: Partition refinement
mcqs.explanation: Partition refinement (also called the table-filling method or iterative refinement) systematically splits groups of states based on distinguishability until equivalent states remain together.

mcqs.question: Two states are distinguishable if:

options.text: They are in the same partition
There exists a string that distinguishes them
They have the same number of transitions

mcqs.correctAnswer: There exists a string that distinguishes them
mcqs.explanation: States are distinguishable if there is at least one input string w such that one state accepts w and the other rejects w. This difference in behavior makes them distinguishable.

mcqs.question: The table-filling method for minimization starts by:

options.text: Marking all pairs of equivalent states
Marking all pairs of distinguishable states

mcqs.correctAnswer: Marking all pairs of distinguishable states
mcqs.explanation: The table-filling algorithm initially marks state pairs where one is accepting and one is non-accepting as distinguishable, then iteratively marks more pairs based on transitions.

mcqs.question: A minimal DFA has the property that:
mcqs.correctAnswer: No two states are equivalent
mcqs.explanation: By definition, a minimal DFA cannot be further reduced, which means no two distinct states are equivalent (indistinguishable). Each state represents a distinct behavior.

mcqs.question: In the partition refinement algorithm, a group of states is split when:

options.text: All states go to the same next state
States in the group go to different groups on some input
The group has more than two states
The group contains the start state

mcqs.correctAnswer: States in the group go to different groups on some input
mcqs.explanation: If two states in the same group have transitions on some input symbol to states in different groups, they must be distinguishable and the group is split accordingly.

mcqs.question: What is an unreachable state?

options.text: A state with no incoming transitions
A state not reachable from the start state

mcqs.correctAnswer: A state not reachable from the start state
mcqs.explanation: Unreachable states cannot be reached from the start state via any sequence of transitions. They should be removed before minimization as they don't affect the language.

mcqs.question: The Myhill-Nerode equivalence relation is based on:
mcqs.correctAnswer: Right-invariant equivalence relation
mcqs.explanation: The Myhill-Nerode relation is right-invariant: if x ≡ y, then for any string z, xz ≡ yz. This property ensures the equivalence classes work with the DFA structure.

mcqs.question: For a complete DFA:

options.text: Every state has at least one transition for each input
Every state has exactly one transition for each input
The DFA has no dead states

mcqs.correctAnswer: Every state has exactly one transition for each input
mcqs.explanation: A complete (or fully specified) DFA has a defined transition for every state and every input symbol. Incomplete DFAs can be completed by adding a dead state.

mcqs.question: The time complexity of DFA minimization using partition refinement is:
mcqs.correctAnswer: O(n log n)
mcqs.explanation: With efficient implementation using queues and appropriate data structures, partition refinement runs in O(n log n) time where n is the number of states.

mcqs.question: Which of these languages has a 2-state minimal DFA?

options.text: {w | w has equal number of a's and b's}

mcqs.correctAnswer: {a^n b^m | n,m ≥ 0}
mcqs.explanation: The language (a*b*) or {a^n b^m | n,m ≥ 0} can be recognized by a 2-state DFA: a start/accepting state that stays on 'a' and transitions to a dead state on 'b', or a simpler structure.

mcqs.question: State equivalence is which type of relation?
mcqs.correctAnswer: Equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, transitive)
mcqs.explanation: State equivalence (indistinguishability) satisfies all three properties: reflexive (every state is equivalent to itself), symmetric (if p≡q then q≡p), and transitive (if p≡q and q≡r then p≡r).

mcqs.question: Before applying minimization, incomplete DFA should be:
mcqs.correctAnswer: Completed by adding dead states
mcqs.explanation: Minimization algorithms typically require complete DFAs. Undefined transitions are handled by adding a dead/trap state that loops to itself.

mcqs.question: If a DFA has n states, the maximum number of distinguishable state pairs is:
mcqs.explanation: With n states, there are n(n-1)/2 possible unordered pairs of distinct states. Each pair can either be distinguishable or indistinguishable.

mcqs.question: The minimization of a DFA:

options.text: Always increases the number of states

mcqs.correctAnswer: Preserves the language recognized
mcqs.explanation: Minimization produces an equivalent DFA - it recognizes exactly the same language as the original, just with fewer states.

mcqs.question: In the table-filling method, a pair (p,q) is marked distinguishable if:

options.text: p and q are in the same current partition
p and q have different numbers of transitions
Some transition from p and q leads to a marked pair

mcqs.correctAnswer: Some transition from p and q leads to a marked pair
mcqs.explanation: If for some input symbol, the transitions from states p and q go to states that are already known to be distinguishable, then p and q themselves are distinguishable.
